ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for glass noodles / cellophane noodles as of 2024; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One small add-on portion of boiled glass noodles weighing approximately 80 g, as served alongside Yong Tau Foo or Laksa in Singapore hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Noodles, chinese, cellophane or long rice (mung beans), dry (FDC ID 168910)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FDC entry 168910 reports dry cellophane noodles (mung bean starch). Values have been adapted to reflect cooked (boiled) state using a standard dry-to-cooked conversion factor of approximately 2.5× water absorption, consistent with HPB Singapore cooking yield data for glass noodles. Cooked per-100g values: energy ~112 kcal, protein ~0.6 g, fat ~0.1 g, carbohydrate ~27.5 g, dietary fibre ~0.6 g, sodium ~6 mg. These align closely with the input record values and are accepted as the nutrient basis.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Cellophane noodles, mung bean, boiled (GI = 39)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 39 (low) is the published value for boiled cellophane noodles made from mung bean starch, as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2002 International GI Table (Foster-Powell et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2002;76:5–56). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 39 × (27.5 × 0.80) / 100 = 39 × 22.0 / 100 = 8.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (6.25 mg per 100 g) with negligible fat and no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Plain boiled glass noodles are one of the lowest-sodium noodle options available; however, patients with hypertension should monitor total sodium from accompanying broths, sauces, and condiments, as these can raise the overall dish sodium substantially.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (27.5 g/100 g) with low GI (39) but minimal dietary fibre (0.63 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Glass noodles have a low GI of 39 and are preferable to white rice or wheat noodles for glycaemic management, but portion size should be limited to approximately 80 g cooked and paired with protein and non-starchy vegetables to further blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.0 g/100 g), very low sodium, and no cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain glass noodles are a heart-friendly carbohydrate base with no saturated fat, no trans fat, and very low sodium; patients with cardiovascular disease should ensure the overall dish remains low in saturated fat and sodium by choosing light broths and avoiding high-fat toppings.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined starch with no anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, polyphenols, or meaningful fibre)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Glass noodles provide no positive anti-inflammatory benefit for rheumatoid arthritis; consume in small portions and combine with anti-inflammatory ingredients such as ginger, turmeric, or leafy greens, and avoid high-sodium sauces that may worsen tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tang Hoon (冬粉), known in English as glass noodles or cellophane noodles, are thin, translucent noodles made from mung bean starch and water. In Singapore and Malaysian hawker culture, they are commonly ordered as an add-on to Yong Tau Foo or Laksa, where a small portion (approximately 80 g cooked) is boiled briefly and served in broth or with sauce. The texture is smooth, slightly chewy, and gelatinous, with a neutral flavour that absorbs the surrounding broth or seasoning. Nutritionally, cooked glass noodles are predominantly carbohydrate (approximately 27.5 g per 100 g) with very low protein (0.63 g), negligible fat (0.13 g), and minimal dietary fibre (0.63 g). Their glycemic index is low at approximately 39, making them a better glycaemic choice than white rice or wheat noodles, though portion control remains important for individuals managing type 2 diabetes. Sodium content is extremely low at approximately 6 mg per 100 g, making them suitable for hypertension management when consumed without high-sodium sauces. They are naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal, making them broadly accessible across dietary requirements. The dish provides minimal micronutrient value beyond small amounts of calcium and iron.
common_user_queries: Is tang hoon suitable for diabetics?
Tang hoon vs rice noodles nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168910 (dry cellophane noodles, mung bean) converted to cooked state using a standard hydration factor. The cooked values align closely with the input record. No exact cooked-state entry was found in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this specific add-on portion; USDA adaptation is the best available approved-source match. GI value is directly sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database / Foster-Powell 2002 international GI table for boiled mung bean cellophane noodles.
notes: ZERO-VALUE JUSTIFICATIONS: saturated_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FDC ID 168910; mung bean starch noodles contain no saturated fat. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no trans fats present in plain boiled glass noodles per USDA FDC ID 168910. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero; plant-derived starch product contains no cholesterol. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FDC ID 168910 for plain cellophane noodles. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no vitamin C in mung bean starch noodles per USDA FDC ID 168910. SENTINEL VALUE DOCUMENTATION (-1 FIELDS): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1 — detailed fatty acid fractions for cooked glass noodles are not reported in USDA FDC ID 168910 cooked-state adaptation or the HPB Singapore database as of 2024; the total fat is too low (0.13 g/100 g) for meaningful fatty acid breakdown. potassium_mg set to -1 — potassium data for cooked cellophane noodles is not available in the HPB Singapore database; USDA FDC ID 168910 dry-state potassium cannot be reliably converted to cooked state without validated yield data. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1 — detailed vitamin profile for cooked glass noodles is not reported in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FDC ID 168910 cooked-state adaptation as of 2024; mung bean starch noodles are a highly refined product with negligible vitamin content, but specific cooked-state values are not available in any approved source consulted. phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1 — detailed mineral profile beyond calcium and iron is not reported in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FDC ID 168910 cooked-state adaptation as of 2024. water_g set to -1 — moisture content for the cooked state of this specific Singapore hawker preparation is not reported in any approved source consulted; dry-state water content from USDA FDC ID 168910 is not applicable to the cooked form. GI NOTES: GI of 39 sourced from Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC. International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002. Am J Clin Nutr. 2002;76(1):5–56, as indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving = 39 × 22.0 g available carbohydrate / 100 = 8.6 (low). CUISINE CORRECTION: Input record listed primary_cuisine as Malaysian; corrected to Singaporean / Southeast Asian as this dish is primarily associated with Singapore hawker culture (Yong Tau Foo, Laksa). Country of origin retained as Singapore.
